articles.title: Denshattack’s surprise ode to Guitar Hero makes for 2026’s best boss fight
articles.excerpt: Denshattack! is full of wild moment, but its unexpected ode to Guitar Hero takes the cake.
articles.body: Denshattack! is so chock-full of ridiculous moments that it’s hard to single out one that stands above the rest. I mean, we’re talking about a game where a train hitches a ride on a runaway Ferris wheel, and that seems like a fairly normal occurrence by the end of the adventure. If I had to pick one moment to sell you on it though, it’s Denshattack ’s unexpected musical boss fight.
articles.author: Giovanni Colantonio

articles.title: We built one AI tutor for five completely different subjects — here's the routing layer that keeps it from falling over
articles.excerpt: Most "AI tutor" apps are a chat window bolted onto a system prompt. We wanted something that actually holds state — knows what you've mastered, adapts difficulty, remembers you tomorrow — across five…
articles.body: Most "AI tutor" apps are a chat window bolted onto a system prompt. We wanted something that actually holds state — knows what you've mastered, adapts difficulty, remembers you tomorrow — across five very different domains: a general subject tutor, exam prep for India's JEE/NEET/UPSC (each with its own official syllabus and negative-marking rules), a language-learning track that goes CEFR A0 through C1, an AI career coach with mock interviews, and a competitive "arena" mode. That range turned out to be the actual engineering problem. Not "can an LLM explain photosynthesis" — every model can do that — but: what happens when you have 60+ distinct AI-backed features, each with different cost/quality tradeoffs, and one of them starts failing at 2am?
articles.author: Devadath H K

articles.title: I Built an AI Memory Agent That Forgets on Purpose — Then Spent Two Days Proving It Actually Works
articles.excerpt: Every "AI memory agent" I looked at before starting this does the same trick: embed every message, dump it in a vector database, retrieve the top-k most similar chunks at query time. That's not memory…
articles.body: Every "AI memory agent" I looked at before starting this does the same trick: embed every message, dump it in a vector database, retrieve the top-k most similar chunks at query time. That's not memory. That's a search index. It never forgets anything, it treats "nice weather today" with the same weight as "I'm allergic to penicillin," and it gets slower and dumber the longer it runs because retrieval gets noisier with every near-duplicate you never clean up. For the Global AI Hackathon Series with Qwen Cloud (Track 1: MemoryAgent), the brief asked for three specific things: efficient storage and retrieval, timely forgetting of outdated information , and recalling critical memories within a limited context window.
articles.author: Tombri Bowei

articles.title: Optimizing RAG at Scale: Chunking, Retrieval, and the Bayesian Search That Cut Latency 40%
articles.excerpt: Optimizing RAG at Scale: Chunking, Retrieval, and the Bayesian Search That Cut Latency 40% How we moved from "semantic search + hope" to a measured, tunable retrieval pipeline with 95% recall@10 Every…
articles.body: Optimizing RAG at Scale: Chunking, Retrieval, and the Bayesian Search That Cut Latency 40% How we moved from "semantic search + hope" to a measured, tunable retrieval pipeline with 95% recall@10 The RAG Reality Check Everyone ships RAG the same way: chunk by 512 tokens, embed with text-embedding-3-small , top-k=5, stuff into context. It works for demos. Then you hit production: Legal contracts: 512 tokens splits clauses mid-sentence API docs: 1000-token chunks drown signal in noise Customer tickets: Conversational context needs overlap, not fixed windows Latency: 500ms embedding + 200ms vector search + 300ms LLM = 1s+ per query We rebuilt our retrieval layer from first principles. Here's what actually moves metrics.
articles.author: Imus

articles.title: Chrome Canary starts testing a big new autofill upgrade
articles.excerpt: Gemini can pull in info from all over your Google account.
articles.body: TL;DR Chrome Canary has a new feature that can fill forms with information from across your Google account. Google says the information filled in this way isn’t visible to anyone and isn’t used to train AI. We don’t know when to expect the feature in stable Chrome. Google’s cooking up an AI-supercharged autofill for Chrome. Chrome Canary’s debuted a new feature called Find and Fill with Gemini, which uses AI to automatically find the information to fill in forms from across different parts of your Google account. As spotted by Windows Report , Find and Fill with Gemini can now be switched on in Chrome Canary via browser settings.
articles.author: Taylor Kerns

articles.title: Data Is the Real Model: Governance, Lineage, and Provenance
articles.excerpt: Part 3 of "Trust the Machine" -> a series on building AI infrastructure that is secure, compliant, and governable by design. The performance of an AI system draws most of the attention: the model, the…
articles.body: Part 3 of "Trust the Machine" -> a series on building AI infrastructure that is secure, compliant, and governable by design. Why data is the bottleneck, not the fuel The performance of an AI system draws most of the attention: the model, the architecture, the benchmark scores. The trustworthiness of that system, however, is determined largely by something less visible: the data behind it. A model is a compressed representation of its training and retrieval data. Whatever that data contains, lacks, or was not permitted to include propagates directly into the model's behavior and into the organization's risk. This has become a practical constraint on adoption, not merely a theoretical concern.
articles.author: Anusha Mukka

articles.title: Why design matters for a web framework: a 7-year evolution
articles.excerpt: Wasp is a full-stack web framework for React and Node.js (imagine Rails for TypeScript, but with frontend included and wired up out-of-the-box), and we've been building it for a little over five years…
articles.body: Wasp is a full-stack web framework for React and Node.js (imagine Rails for TypeScript, but with frontend included and wired up out-of-the-box), and we've been building it for a little over five years (as a full-time thing). For almost all of that time, we saw design and "branding" as something we shouldn't spend too much time on. A framework is mostly code, we thought: you drive it from a CLI and your editor, there is little visual to look at, and these days an agent reads the docs for you half the time anyway. The most loved frameworks kind of prove the point, with Django and Rails famous and famously plain. Turns out we were wrong, though not in the way you'd expect.
articles.author: Matija Sosic

It’s a smattering of the publisher 2K’s biggest games of the 2010s, including BioShock Infinite, Risk of Rain 2, Borderlands 3, and XCOM: Enemy Unknown . The bundle will be available until August 7th, though the bundle page notes that keys for some games are already starting to run low. articles.title: The PlayStation replica ornament is an homage to a great, yet fragile console
articles.excerpt: The ornament requires three 1.5-volt coin batteries, which are included in the box. | Photo: Cameron Faulkner/The Verge You probably know the signature PlayStation boot sound. Did you know that it's t…
articles.body: The ornament requires three 1.5-volt coin batteries, which are included in the box. | Photo: Cameron Faulkner/The Verge You probably know the signature PlayStation boot sound . Did you know that it's technically a multi-part chime? There's the synthy section where "Sony Computer Entertainment" shows onscreen with a white background. You only get to the next screen with the echo-y chimes and the color-filled PlayStation logo if your console recognizes the disc. How badly I wanted my sorta-broken console to get to that second screen. My console's disc spindle came apart pretty early into me owning it. As an eight-year-old kid, fixing the spindle was the riskiest undertaking of my life yet.
articles.author: Cameron Faulkner

articles.title: I Taught a Security Camera to Say "I Don't Know" — and It Got More Useful, Not Less
articles.excerpt: Somewhere around day four of building a doorbell-camera AI for a hackathon, Every doorbell camera on the market answers one question: was there That's the wrong question. The right question — the one…
articles.body: Somewhere around day four of building a doorbell-camera AI for a hackathon, I caught my own system lying to me. Not maliciously — it was just guessing, the way all of us do when we don't want to say "I don't know" out loud. The fix for that turned out to be more interesting than anything else in the build, so this post is mostly about that, with some genuinely funny/painful bug stories along the way. The problem nobody built a camera to solve Every doorbell camera on the market answers one question: was there motion? That's the wrong question. The right question — the one a human actually wants answered at 11pm when their phone buzzes — is should I care about this? Cameras don't answer that, so people train themselves to swipe the notification away without looking.
articles.author: Daniel Ainoko

articles.title: It’s the beginning of the end for the Pixel 6 and Pixel 6 Pro
articles.excerpt: Google’s latest Android 17 Beta quietly marks the sunset of Pixel 6 support.
articles.body: TL;DR Google released Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 today, and the Pixel 6 and Pixel 6 Pro aren’t supported. The phones first launched in October 2021, and Google later extended their software support to five years. It’s been almost three years since the last time we had a Pixel left behind like this, with the Pixel 5 on Android 14 QPR1 Beta 1. Moments ago, Google released its latest testing-track build for Android’s enthusiast community, with Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 . And as we look forward to this software ultimately arriving as Google’s 2026 Q4 Android release, not every phone will be coming along for the journey: This is the end of the line for the Pixel 6 and Pixel 6 Pro . Google launched the Pixel 6 phones back on October 19, 2021, ahead of sales beginning on October 28.
articles.author: Stephen Schenck

articles.title: Tesla ‘Full Self-Driving’ got me a speeding ticket, and Tesla killed the fix
articles.excerpt: I got a speeding ticket this weekend while using Tesla’s Full Self-Driving. The car was driving itself in Standard mode on FSD when it took me up to 78 km/h in a 50 km/h zone. A police officer coming…
articles.body: I got a speeding ticket this weekend while using Tesla’s Full Self-Driving. The car was driving itself in Standard mode on FSD when it took me up to 78 km/h in a 50 km/h zone. A police officer coming the other way clocked me on radar and pulled me over on the spot. I’d turned FSD on less than two minutes earlier.

articles.title: Scientists discover mystery molecule on Pluto and Saturn's moon Titan: 'We cannot say what it is'
articles.excerpt: Despite being separated by billions of miles, Saturn's moon Titan and the dwarf planet Pluto share an unexplained surface signature.
articles.body: Separated by billions of miles and shaped by very different environments, Saturn's smoggy moon Titan and the dwarf planet Pluto have, on paper, little in common. Titan is a geologically active world cloaked in an atmosphere denser than Earth's, its landscape shaped by seas of liquid methane and vast dunes built from organic particles that rain out of its skies. Pluto, by contrast, is a frigid outpost with a tenuous atmosphere hovering above an icy, frozen surface. Yet, observations from the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) have revealed these two disparate worlds share an identical, unexplained infrared signature on their surfaces — a sign that similar chemistry may be unfolding on both worlds despite their stark differences, scientists say.
articles.author: Sharmila Kuthunur

articles.excerpt: An aircraft powered by a megawatt-class hybrid-electric engine developed in collaboration with NASA and built by GE Aerospace, demonstrated flight of an innovation that can inform new generations of f…
articles.body: 4 min read Preparations for Next Moonwalk Simulations Underway (and Underwater) A modified Saab 340B aircraft in flight powered in part by a hybrid electric system built by GE Aerospace, along with NASA, BETA Technologies, and Boeing. GE Aerospace An aircraft powered by a megawatt-class hybrid-electric engine developed in collaboration with NASA and built by GE Aerospace, demonstrated flight of an innovation that can inform new generations of fuel-saving aircraft power systems. Mounted to a Saab 340B aircraft, the engine flew at Farnborough International Air Show in the United Kingdom. It was the public debut of a system that has in recent months made historic test flights, becoming the first hybrid electric-powered aircraft to fly above 30,000 feet.

articles.title: 50 years after Viking 1's historic Mars landing, NASA is looking to the Red Planet's skies
articles.excerpt: NASA's Viking 1 mission blazed a crucial trail with its Mars landing on July 20, 1976. Now, half a century later, that trail leads up into the Red Planet's skies.
articles.body: Half a century after first putting hardware down on Mars, NASA is looking to explore the Red Planet's skies. On July 20, 1976, the Viking 1 lander touched down in the western reaches of Chryse Planitia (the "Golden Plain"), a large circular landform that lies 22.5 degrees north of the Martian equator. It was the first-ever Mars landing for NASA. Viking 1, along with its twin Viking 2, went on to conduct the first fully successful mission on the Red Planet's surface. (The Soviet Union's Mars 3 probe survived its touchdown attempt on Dec. 2, 1971 but died less than two minutes later.) And those missions have left a rich and intriguing legacy. The first color image ever captured on the surface of Mars. NASA's Viking 1 lander took this photo on July 21, 1976.
articles.author: Mike Wall

articles.title: 'Stranger Things' at 10: The Netflix streaming icon that made it cool to like science again
articles.excerpt: Stranger Things changed the face of streaming, but it also had a significant impact on piquing the younger audience's interest in science.
articles.body: In July 2016, "Stranger Things" stormed its way into the pantheon of pop culture greats. Finding the sweet spot of '80s nostalgia, horror, and sci-fi, the Duffer Brothers' series established itself as one of Netflix's flagship shows and a favourite water cooler topic for close to a decade. Much like "Jurassic Park" encouraged an entire generation to suddenly view paleontology through a new set of eyes, the same can be said about "Stranger Things" and science. Seriously, how many of us discovered the many-worlds theory because of the concept of the Upside Down on the show?
articles.author: Sergio Pereira

articles.title: Twice in a row: SpaceX rocket aborts launch at last second again (video)
articles.excerpt: SpaceX suffered its second last-second abort in a row this morning (July 20), this time during an attempted Falcon 9 launch of Starlink broadband satellites.
articles.body: That's two last-second launch aborts in a row for SpaceX. One of the company's Falcon 9 rockets tried to loft 24 Starlink internet satellites from Vandenberg Space Force Base in California this morning (July 20), but the attempt was aborted just as the first-stage engines started firing up. Something similar happened on Thursday afternoon (July 16), when SpaceX attempted to launch the 13th test flight of its Starship megarocket. A SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket tries to launch 24 Starlink satellites from Vandenberg Space Force Base on July 20, 2026. The launch aborted at the last second. (Image credit: SpaceX) The two events likely have little in common other than a proximity in time.
articles.author: Mike Wall

articles.title: Adobe’s ‘natural look’ camera app embraces generative AI
articles.excerpt: Welcome to the “what is a photo” debate, Adobe. | Image: Adobe Adobe's experimental camera app has taken an unexpected turn. After Project Indigo was launched last year to provide a "more natural (SLR…
articles.body: Welcome to the “what is a photo” debate, Adobe. | Image: Adobe Adobe's experimental camera app has taken an unexpected turn. After Project Indigo was launched last year to provide a "more natural (SLR-like) look" for iPhone photography, the Indigo camera app is now being updated with a suite of generative AI tools. And the change doesn't rely upon Adobe's own Firefly AI models. Adobe describes the new "AI Playground" tooling suite as an experiment and says there's a button that allows users to opt out and continue using the app as before. Free access to the suite with no sign-on requirement is being tested with a small percentage of Indigo users over the next few weeks. "Our goal is to learn how people … Read the full story at The Verge.
